Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'm 29, so I've been around the block with university life and I know what works for me. I'd typically block out about 4-5 hours a day across the week, mostly in the mornings when my head is clearest. I'd read through materials, take notes by hand, and then type them up later. The structure was there, but looking back, maybe I was just going through the motions without really absorbing things deeply.
Q2: I leaned toward regularity because chaos stresses me out, but deadlines definitely shifted things around. When assignments were due, I'd compress other activities and focus more intensely on what needed submitting. My extracurriculars sometimes pulled me away from my ideal schedule, so I had to adapt. Overall though, I tried to maintain a baseline rhythm throughout the semester.
Q3: Usually it was habit more than anything else—I'd log in first thing in the morning with my coffee just to see if there were announcements or new materials. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or an upcoming deadline that prompted me to check in. Honestly, a lot of the time I was just scrolling through without much purpose, feeling kind of bored but doing it anyway because it felt like I should.
Q4: Yeah, it became pretty automatic after the first few weeks. I'd wake up, check my phone, then sit at my desk and open the LMS before doing anything else. It was like checking email—just part of the morning routine. I don't know if that made me more engaged, but it definitely made me consistent with logging in at least.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my bread and butter since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download the PDFs and annotate them heavily. The discussion forums were also useful because seeing other people's perspectives sometimes helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed written feedback on assignments too.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the video content, to be honest. I'd let videos play in the background while doing other things, but I rarely watched them actively. They just don't stick for me the way reading does. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because they felt like busywork when I was already spending so much time on the core materials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If something helped me understand a concept I'd need to apply later, I was all in. I also valued anything that let me engage at my own pace—reading and writing activities where I could pause and think. Anything that felt rushed or superficial just made me feel more bored.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like extras I could skip if I was tired or busy, even if they might have been helpful. Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them no matter what. I think I sometimes dismissed optional stuff too quickly because I was already putting in so many hours and felt like I was doing enough.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with printed notes and handwritten summaries. I'd download everything I needed and then work away from the computer. I also used some external textbooks and resources I found on my own that weren't part of the course materials. The LMS was more like a content delivery system for me than where I actually learned.
Q10: There were entire evenings where I'd sit with my notes and textbooks, reviewing and rewriting concepts, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss course content with friends from my extracurricular activities who were in similar programs. Those conversations helped me process ideas, but they're completely invisible to any tracking.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF, maybe open it once in the LMS to mark it as accessed, and then spend hours with the printed version over the following days. My real engagement with the material happened with highlighters and pens in hand, not clicking through screens. The LMS would show one quick access, but that doesn't capture the depth of my interaction.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My stress levels were through the roof even though I don't feel particularly anxious normally. I just felt overwhelmed by the volume across all 17 courses I was taking. There were days when I'd log in, stare at the screen, and feel completely bored and disconnected despite knowing I needed to engage. The mindfulness exercises they offered helped a bit, but the underlying exhaustion was still there.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—check boxes, submit what was due, but not really dig deeper. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes because I didn't have the energy to wrestle with them. I'd tell myself I'd come back to it later, but later rarely came.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through discussion posts or rewatching videos, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was physically present but mentally checked out. It's that bored feeling—I was doing the actions of studying without actually learning anything meaningful in those moments.
Q15: I postponed the exam review materials way too long because they felt intimidating. I knew they were crucial, but I kept telling myself I'd get to them after finishing other assignments. The procrastination was partly about low motivation—I just couldn't muster the enthusiasm to tackle the hard stuff when easier tasks were available. That probably contributed to my poor exam performance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation since my internal drive was pretty low this semester. I'd ramp up my LMS activity in the days before something was due, making sure everything was submitted on time. My completion rates stayed high because of deadlines, but the quality of my engagement was definitely better when I wasn't rushing. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did make me want to engage more with similar materials to improve. But feedback often came late, so by the time I received it, I'd already moved on mentally. Positive feedback gave me a small boost, but it was temporary. Honestly, my motivation was so low that even good feedback didn't sustain my engagement for long.
Q18: The course structure gave me a roadmap, which I appreciated because I need that external organization. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me allocate my 30 hours of weekly study time. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes—like I was just following a checklist without really understanding why certain things were sequenced the way they were.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content made things harder for me since that's not how I learn best. I wished there were more detailed written explanations as alternatives. On the positive side, the discussion forums were well-organized and easy to navigate. The assignment submission process was straightforward, which reduced friction when I was already feeling low on motivation.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I logged in constantly, watched videos play in the background, participated in discussions—but my actual submitted work didn't reflect that activity level. Sometimes high activity is just nervous energy or going through motions without productive output. You can be very present in a system without that presence translating into quality work.
Q21: That's not really my experience since I had high activity but poor exam results. But I can see how someone who studies efficiently offline could appear disengaged in the LMS while actually mastering the material. The system only captures what happens inside it, not the thinking and learning that happens elsewhere. Different learning styles probably explain a lot of that pattern.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, how often, what they click on. That's useful for identifying who's completely absent. But logs miss the quality of attention, whether someone is actually thinking critically or just scrolling. They miss offline study entirely, and they can't capture whether high activity reflects genuine engagement or just anxious clicking around.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when concepts connect in your head, when you want to discuss ideas with others. For me this semester, I was going through the motions—completing everything, logging in constantly—but that deeper engagement was missing. I was present but not truly engaged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials away from screens, or the conversations I had with peers outside the platform. It wouldn't capture my mental state—how bored and unmotivated I felt despite doing everything I was supposed to do. It also can't show that I'm a 29-year-old with life experience trying to balance multiple responsibilities, which affects how I approach learning differently than a younger student might.
